articles.title: Open Thread
articles.content:
登录
创建新账户
大多为法学教授 | 有时逆向思维 | 常持自由意志主义观点 | 始终保持独立
你在想什么？
尤金·沃洛赫
|
2026年7月19日 凌晨3:00
下一篇："自由市场"比"资本主义"更受欢迎
尤金·沃洛赫是斯坦福大学胡佛研究所的托马斯·M·西贝尔高级研究员，同时也是加州大学洛杉矶分校法学院的加里·T·施瓦茨荣誉杰出法学教授和杰出研究教授。当然，他在这里的帖子（就像其他博主的观点一样）仅代表个人观点，不代表任何机构的立场。他还是"自由言论不沉默"播客的联合主持人。
显示评论 (0)
C. 贾雷特·迪特莱 和 库尔特·霍夫曼

articles.title: The "Free Market" is More Popular than "Capitalism"
articles.content:
一项最新的Echelon Insights民调发现，"自由市场经济"比"资本主义"更受欢迎。53%的受访者表示对前者持"非常"或"有些"正面看法，而只有12%持负面看法。相比之下，"资本主义"获得49%的正面评价，而两个负面类别的合计达到29%（是"自由市场经济"负面比例的两倍多）。

其他民调也显示类似结果。例如，最新的盖洛普调查显示，54%的人对"资本主义"持正面态度，但81%的人对"自由企业"持同样看法。2025年12月由著名保守派民调专家斯科特·拉斯穆森主持的纳波利坦新闻服务民调发现，64%的受访者对"自由市场"持好感，但只有53%的人对"资本主义"持同样看法。Reason-Rupe民调早在2015年就得出类似结果。在Echelon和一些其他民调中，"资本主义"在年轻人中的评价异常差，而"自由市场"则基本没有这个问题。

这种因问题措辞不同而产生回应差异的现象并不令人惊讶。在许多议题上都存在这种情况。在一个政治无知普遍存在的世界里，许多人对各种问题并没有经过深思熟虑的看法，很容易受到问题措辞方式的影响。许多人在其他方面也存在困惑。拉斯穆森的民调甚至发现，48%对"社会主义"持好感的人同时也对"资本主义"持好感！然而，否定资本主义是社会主义意识形态的核心要素。如果资本主义运转良好，那就强烈意味着社会主义并非如此（反之亦然）。

"自由市场"比"资本主义"获得更正面反应并不完全令人意外。前者让人直觉联想到自由和选择。后者则让人联想到"资本"和"资本家"，这些词汇让我们中的许多人想到贪婪、自私的商业利益和富人。"资本主义"一词最初是由19世纪的社会主义者创造和推广的，这并非偶然！

在一个更好的世界里，人们对思想的反应不应该如此容易受到措辞变化的影响。但在现实世界中，自由意志主义者和其它自由市场倡导者（注意我说的是"自由市场倡导者"而非"资本主义倡导者"！）应该注意到这一点。我们应该尽可能使用"自由市场"及类似措辞（如"自由企业"），同时避免使用"资本主义"。我自己多年来基本上一直在遵循这一做法。我敦促其他人也这样做。

《"自由市场"比"资本主义"更受欢迎》首发于Reason.com。
articles.summary:
A recent Echelon Insights poll finds that the "free market economy" is more popular than "capitalism." 53% of respondents say they have a "very" or "somewhat" favorable view of the former, while only 12% have an very or somewhat unfavorable view. By contrast, "capitalism" is viewed very or somewhat favorably by 49%, while the two unfavorable categories combine for 29% (more than the double percentage for "free market economy").

Other polls show similar results. For example, a recent Gallup survey finds 54% have a positive  view of "capitalism," but 81% say the same of "free enterprise." A December 2025 Napolitan News Service poll conducted by prominent conservative pollster Scott Rasmussen found 64% of respondents have a favorable view of the "free market," but only 53% say that of "capitalism." A Reason-Rupe poll reached a similar result back in 2015. In Echelon and some other polls, "capitalism" polls unusually badly among younger people, while "free market" mostly does not.

Such variation in responses based on question wording isn't surprising. It occurs with respect to many issues. In a world of widespread political ignorance, many people don't have carefully thought out views on a variety of issues, and can easily be influenced by the way questions are worded. Many are also confused in other ways. Rasmussen's poll even found that 48% of those who have favorable opinion of "socialism" also have a favorable opinion of "capitalism"! Yet rejection of capitalism is a central element of socialist ideology. If capitalism works well, that strongly implies socialism does not (and vice versa).

It is not entirely surprising that "free market" generates more favorable reactions than "capitalism." The former has positive intuitive associations with freedom and choice. The latter has associations with "capital" and "capitalists," which words make many of us think of greedy, selfish business interests and rich people. It is no accident that "capitalism" was first used and popularized by nineteenth century socialists!

In a better world, people's reactions to ideas should not be so susceptible to variations in wording. But in the world we actually live in, libertarians and other free market advocates (notice I did not say "capitalism advocates"!) should take note. As much as possible, we should use "free market" and similar wording (e.g. - "free enterprise"), while abjuring "capitalism." I myself have largely been following that practice for many years. I urge others to do the same.

The post The "Free Market" is More Popular than "Capitalism" appeared first on Reason.com.

articles.title: Is The Hawaii Supreme Court's "Aloha Spirit" Consistent With A Republican Form Of Government?
articles.content:
夏威夷于1959年作为第50个州加入合众国。夏威夷人民的经历与其他49个州人民的经历截然不同。美利坚合众国于1776年通过推翻君主制而建立，而夏威夷直到1893年仍处于君主统治之下。十九世纪期间，当美国各州和领地享有持有和携带武器的权利时，夏威夷却没有这种保护。杰克逊大法官在她的沃尔福德异议意见中回顾了这段历史：

“自作为主权王国以来，夏威夷从未允许在其领土上广泛携带火器。1833年，夏威夷王国国王卡美哈美哈三世禁止持有'危险武器'。《夏威夷群岛宪法与法律汇编》，卡美哈美哈三世统治时期确立，第163页（1842年）（1934年重印）（针对任何'刀、剑杖或任何其他危险武器'的持有）。夏威夷在1898年被吞并为美国领地前后，都保持着严格管制武器的传统。”

关于杰克逊大法官是否应该援引种族主义的《吉姆·克劳法》展开了广泛讨论，但援引夏威夷君主法律的问题更加棘手。一位国王颁布携带武器禁令为何具有关联性？英王乔治三世和托马斯·盖奇将军曾在马萨诸塞湾殖民地实施武器禁令。这种权利侵犯引发了独立战争，并激发了《第二修正案》的诞生。没有任何理由援引卡美哈美哈国王的法律来解释《第二修正案》。

夏威夷最高法院声称基于该州独特的法律历史解释联邦宪法。夏威夷最高法院的埃丁斯大法官在《州诉威尔逊案》中写道，“阿罗哈精神”应启发其法院如何解读联邦宪法：

“在夏威夷，阿罗哈精神激发宪法解释。参见Sunapo案，153夏威夷州第363页，537 P.3d第1210页（埃丁斯法官附和意见）。当本法院代表人民行使'权力，履行我们的责任、义务和服务'时，我们'可以思考并融入生命力量，并对阿罗哈精神给予考虑'。阿罗哈精神与联邦强制的让公民在日常活动中携带致命武器的生活方式相冲突。夏威夷群岛的历史不包括一个武装人员在社区中走动以应对他人致命威胁的社会。参见夏威夷州宪法第九条第十款（'碎桨之法……应是该州对公共安全关切的独特而鲜活的象征'）。”

最高法院正确地驳回了埃丁斯大法官对宪法的解读：

“正如麦科唐纳案中多数意见所解释的，《第二修正案》在美国各地具有相同的含义。561 U.S.第784-785页，130 S.Ct.第3020页。它不能因为夏威夷的'阿罗哈精神'而退让，正如它不能向'大苹果精神'（布鲁恩案）或'风城精神'（麦科唐纳案）屈服一样。它以相同的方式适用于我们的第50个州（约8%的成年人拥有枪支）和我们的第49个州（该数字约为59%）。仅仅是地方态度既不能缩小也不能扩大通过第十四修正案适用于各州的基本权利法案保障的含义。”

“阿罗哈精神”——无论那是什么——产生于与大陆各州截然不同的法律、政治和社会环境。一位宣誓效忠联邦宪法的州最高法院法官，能否基于一种与美国历史和传统不一致的“精神”作出裁决？更直白地说，夏威夷最高法院的'阿罗哈精神'是否符合共和形式的政府？马萨诸塞州最高司法法院的法官能否援引盖奇将军没收武器的行为来支持该州的严格枪支管制法律？是否存在'红袜精神'？

不过，我认为问题更加深刻。埃丁斯大法官似乎在说，夏威夷的法律体系与大陆发展起来的法律体系格格不入。我认为埃丁斯大法官（或他的聊天机器人）并没有完全考虑这种立场的含义。事实上，最高法院有一桩1901年关于另一个领地的相关案例：

“如果这些领地居住着与我们宗教不同、习俗不同、法律不同、税收方法不同、思维方式不同的外星种族，政府和司法的英美原则管理可能在暂时无法实现，问题立即出现：是否应该在一定时期内作出重大让步，以便最终实现我们自己的理论，并将宪法下自由政府的福祉扩展到他们。我们拒绝认定宪法中有任何禁止此类行动的内容。”

当然，这段话来自Downes v. Bidwell案，即“岛屿案件”之一。

夏威夷最高法院辩称，第50个州的历史与《第二修正案》不符。也许宪法与'阿罗哈精神'不符。

随着各州及其法院继续走极端，我们需要更多地思考保证条款。一个一直萦绕在我心头的问题是：一个明确采用社会主义制度的州是否符合共和形式的政府？

最高法院在芭芭拉案中回避了“入侵”问题，但这类问题的可诉性不会消失。
articles.summary:
Hawaii was admitted as the 50th state in 1959. The experience of the Hawaiian people was very, very different from the experience of the people in the other 49 states. The United States of America was established by overthrowing a monarch in 1776. Hawaii remained under the control of a monarch until 1893. During the Nineteenth Century, as the American states and territories lived with a right to keep and bear arms, Hawaii had no such protection. Justice Jackson recounts this history in her Wolford dissent:

Since its time as a sovereign kingdom, Hawaii has never permitted the widespread carrying of firearms in its territory. In 1833, King Kamehameha III of the Kingdom of Hawaii prohibited the possession of "dangerous weapon[s]." Translation of the Constitution and Laws of the Hawaiian Islands, Established in the Reign of Kamehameha III 163 (1842) (reprint 1934) (targeting possession of any "knife, sword-cane, or any other dangerous weapon"). Hawaii maintained this tradition of strictly regulating weapons both before and after it was annexed as a U. S. territory in 1898.

There was an extended discussion of whether Justice Jackson should have relied on racist Jim Crow laws, but the citation to Hawaiian monarchical laws is even more problematic. Why is it relevant that a King imposed a prohibition on carrying arms. King George III, and General Thomas Gage, imposed prohibitions on arms in the Massachusetts Bay colony. That violation of rights triggered the Revolutionary War, and inspired the Second Amendment. There is no reason to cite King Kamehameha's laws as a means to interpret the Second Amendment.

The Hawaii Supreme Court has stated that it interprets the federal Constitution based on the unique legal history of the state. Justice Eddins of the Hawaii Supreme Court wrote in State v. Wilson that the "Aloha Spirit" should inspire how his court reads the federal Constitution:

In Hawai'i, the Aloha Spirit inspires constitutional interpretation. See Sunoco, 153 Hawai'i at 363, 537 P.3d at 1210 (Eddins, J., concurring). When this court exercises "power on behalf of the people and in fulfillment of [our] responsibilities, obligations, and service to the people" we "may contemplate and reside with the life force and give consideration to the 'Aloha Spirit.'"  The spirit of Aloha clashes with a federally-mandated lifestyle that lets citizens walk around with deadly weapons during day-to-day activities. The history of the Hawaiian Islands does not include a society where armed people move about the community to possibly combat the deadly aims of others. See Haw. Const. art. IX, § 10 ("The law of the splintered paddle … shall be a unique and living symbol of the State's concern for public safety.").

The Supreme Court rightly rejected Justice Eddins's reading of the Constitution:

As the plurality explained in McDonald, the Second Amendment has the same meaning in all parts of the United States. 561 U.S. at 784–785, 130 S.Ct. 3020. It cannot give way to "the spirit of Aloha" in Hawaii, any more than it can yield to the spirit of the Big Apple (Bruen) or the Windy City (McDonald). It applies in the same way to our 50th State (where about 8% of adults possess guns) and our 49th State (where the figure is roughly 59%).Merely local attitudes can neither shrink nor inflate the meaning of fundamental Bill of Rights guarantees that apply to the States through the Fourteenth Amendment.

The "Aloha Spirit," whatever that is, developed from a very different legal, political, and social set of circumstances than the states on the mainland. Can a state supreme court justice, who takes an oath to the federal Constitution, base his decisions on a "spirit" that is inconsistent with the American history and tradition? Stated more bluntly, is the Hawaii Supreme Court's "Aloha Spirit" consistent with a Republican form of government? Could a justice on the Massachusetts Supreme Judicial Court cite General Gage's seizure of firearms to support the Commonwealth's strict gun control laws? Is there a "Red Sox Spirit"?

Still, I think the problem goes even deeper. Justice Eddins seems to be saying that the Hawaiian legal system is alien from the legal system that developed on the mainland. I don't think Justice Eddins (or his chatbot) has fully thought through the implications of that position. Indeed, there is a relevant Supreme Court case from 1901 about another territory:

If those possessions are inhabited by alien races, differing from us in religion, customs, laws, methods of taxation, and modes of thought, the administration of government and justice according to Anglo-Saxon principles may for a time be impossible, and the question at once arises whether large concessions ought not to be made for a time, that ultimately our own theories may be carried out and the blessings of a free government under the Constitution extended to them. We decline to hold that there is anything in the Constitution to forbid such action.

Of course, this passage comes from Downes v. Bidwell, one of the Insular Cases.

The Hawaii Supreme Court argued the 50th state's history was inconsistent with the Second Amendment. Perhaps the Constitution is inconsistent with the "Aloha Spirit."

As states and their courts continue to go off the deep end, we need to give more thought to the Guarantee Clause. One question that has been on my mind: would a state that adopts an expressly socialist system of government be consistent with a Republican form of government?

The Supreme Court ducked the "invasion" issue in Barbara, but the justiciability of these sorts of questions are not going anywhere.

The post Is The Hawaii Supreme Court's "Aloha Spirit" Consistent With A Republican Form Of Government? appeared first on Reason.com.

articles.title: Trump's Harmful and Unjust New Restrictions on Foreign Students
articles.content:
特朗普政府最近颁布了新的、极度限制性的规定，约束在美高校就读的外国学生。我的卡托研究所同事大卫·比尔是移民政策领域的权威专家，他撰写了一篇有用的文章，总结了这些政策变化及其危害性质：

此前，持有F签证的国际学生、持有J签证的访问学者和持有I签证的外国媒体记者获准在"身份有效期内"居留——即只要他们保持合法身份或遵守签证项目规则即可。国土安全部的最终规定用固定居留期限取代了这一做法，国际学生和访问学者的最长居留期为四年。但该规定所做的远不止结束无限期居留。它充斥着大量史无前例的限制措施，而这些措施没有任何法律依据。

——学生第一年不能换专业或转校，研究生则永远不能。该规定本身的摘要明确指出：

对于F-1学生改变学业目标或转入经SEVP认证的学校，要求学生在其最初签发I-20表格或继任表格的学校完成第一学年的学习，除非获得SEVP授权；禁止研究生及以上学历的F-1学生在学习期间的任何时候改变学业目标。

本科生在一年后可解除此限制。研究生则永远无法解除，除非获得SEVP批准。在博士第四年想改变论文方向？那就和第一天就违反身份规定的后果一样。

——你永远不能在同级完成第二个学位，也不能降级就读。同样来自摘要：

要求任何完成某一教育级别项目的非移民，只能作为F-1学生在更高教育级别开始另一个项目，禁止在作为F-1学生期间降至同级或更低教育级别。

读完一个硕士想再读另一个不同领域的硕士？违法。读完博士想学一门实用的副学士学位课程？违法。

——国土安全部对担心需要双学位的人的真正回应是：提前规划，否则后果自负。评论者指出，这将对需要两个学位才能开展研究的跨学科领域学生造成毁灭性打击。国土安全部的回答是：

本规定不妨碍需要双学位的学生通过提前规划同时注册两个专业来实现目标。例如，本规定不禁止某人通过一个I-20表格在同一机构完成J.D./M.B.A.项目，该表格注明了完成双学位所需的更长时间。

J.D.和M.B.A.根本不是专业的例子，而是资质类型，因此撰写此规定的人对学生在高等教育中的实际学习情况一无所知。更重要的是，许多人到美国大学时并非从第一天起就知道自己需要攻读联合学位。国土安全部对这一现实的回应是：你应该提前知道。

正如大卫所解释的，新规定还使外国学生在毕业后在美国找工作并留下来变得更加困难。

从教育和经济角度来看，这些新规定毫无道理。正如大卫所指出的，某些学位由于各种原因需要四年以上才能完成，学生换专业、增设第二专业等都有充分的理由。这一切的真正目的不过是减少外国学生的数量。

这反过来会随着时间的推移对美国经济造成严重损害，特别是与特朗普采取的其他反外国学生措施相结合。约翰斯·霍普金斯大学教授迈克尔·克莱门斯——世界领先的移民经济学家之一——最近发表了一篇有价值的文章，概述了这些损害：

几代以来，来自世界各地的学生一直在为美国大学提供动力，美国是他们压倒性的首选目的地。这些学生不仅维持着每年430亿美元的高等教育服务出口产业。许多人毕业后留下来，成为高技能科技和工程人才的关键来源，从而推动美国经济的创新和增长。

这一体系现在正在崩溃。白宫已经颁布了一系列大规模限制和排斥国际学生的政策……

如果这些政策持续下去，将限制美国经济科技和工程人才的关键供应，降低生产率，从长远来看减缓经济增长。利用经济学研究文献中不完美但最佳的可用估计，我们预测国际学生流入量持续减少三分之一所带来的损失。在十年期间，美国实际GDP将比不减少时更小——按当前GDP计算，年损失在2400亿至4810亿美元之间。年损失相当于抹去威斯康星州或南卡罗来纳州的整个经济体。

三分之一减少量是根据这些最新规定之前已实施的特朗普政策的影响推算的。

克莱门斯的文章详细阐述了这一估计的基础，并指出他们未考虑的其他损害。例如，许多美国大学严重依赖外国学生的学费，这些学费通常用于补贴美国本土学生的助学金。

新政策还叠加在特朗普违宪且有害的努力之上——驱逐从事政府不认可的 政治言论的外国学生，这一政策除了其他缺陷外，还对学术自由构成威胁。

此外，新政策将使成千上万原本可以入学的学生失去宝贵的教育和职业机会。而理由仅仅是他们投胎到了错误的地方、错误的父母。保守派正确地谴责大学录取中的种族和民族优惠待遇是不公正的和反精英主义的，因为它们根据人们无法控制的道德任意因素——祖先来评判申请者。在我之前的文章中（见此处和此处），我概述了为什么移民限制——包括对外国学生的歧视——出于类似原因是不公正的，但程度要大得多，因为其影响更大。从低级别学校而非比如说哈佛毕业（种族平权行动的影响）与被允许在美国学习与被彻底排斥之间的差异是无法相比的。

《特朗普对外国学生有害且不公正的新限制》首次发表于Reason.com。
articles.summary:
The Trump administration recently adopted new, gratuitously restrictive, rules constraining foreign students at US universities. My Cato Institute colleague David Bier, a leading expert on immigration policy, has a helpful piece summarizing the policy changes, and their pernicious nature:

Previously, international students on F visas, exchange visitors on J visas, and foreign media on I visas were granted admission for their "duration of status"—that is, for as long as they were in status or following the rules of the visa programs. DHS's final rule replaces that with a fixed period of entry of no more than four years (for international students and exchange visitors). But it does far more than end open-ended admission. Littered throughout it is a set of unprecedented restrictions with no statutory basis.

  • Students can't change majors or transfer schools in year one—and graduate students can't ever. The rule's own summary states it plainly:

For F‑1 students changing educational objectives or transferring to an SEVP-certified school, requiring that the student complete his or her first academic year of a program of study at the school that initially issued his or her Form I‑20 or successor form, unless an exception is authorized by SEVP; Prohibiting F‑1 students at the graduate education level or above from changing educational objectives at any point during a program of study.

Undergrads get one year before this restriction lifts. Graduate students never get it lifted at all, absent a SEVP exception. Change your mind about your PhD topic in year four, and you're the same as someone violating status on day one.

  • You can't complete a second degree at the same level, or step down a level, ever. Also from the summary:

Requiring any nonimmigrant who has completed a program at one educational level to only be allowed to begin another program at a higher educational level as an F‑1 student and prohibiting a change to the same or a lower educational level while an F‑1 student.

Finish a master's and want a second master's in a different field? Illegal. Finish a PhD and want to pick up an associate's degree in something practical? Illegal.

  • DHS's real response to people worried about the need to double-major: plan ahead, or too bad. Commenters pointed out this would devastate students in interdisciplinary fields who need two degrees to do their work. DHS's answer:

this rule does not prevent students who need double majors to achieve their goals from planning ahead and enrolling in both at the same time. For example, nothing in this rule prevents someone from doing a J.D./M.B.A. program at one institution with one I‑20 indicating the program end date that accounts for the longer time it takes to complete the double major.

A J.D. and M.B.A. are not even examples of majors at all, as opposed to types of credentials, so whoever wrote this rule doesn't have the slightest idea what students are even doing in higher education. More to the point, though, many people arrive at a US university not already knowing they'll need to pursue a joint degree on day one. DHS's answer to that reality is: you should have known in advance.

As David explains, the new rule also makes it more difficult for foreign students to find jobs and stay in the US after graduation.

From an educational and economic point of view, the new rules make no sense. As David notes, some degrees take more than four years to complete, for various reasons, and there are often good reasons for students to change fields, add additional majors, and the like. The real purpose of all of this is just to reduce the number of foreign students.

That, in turn would, over time, do serious damage to the US economy, especially in combination with other anti-foreign student measures adopted by Trump. Johns Hopkins Prof. Michael Clemens - one of the world's leading immigration economists - recently published a valuable article outlining many of these harms:

For generations, students from around the world have fueled universities in the United States, their top destination by far. Those students don't just sustain a $43 billion export industry in higher education services per year. Many remain after graduation as a key source of high-skill science and engineering workers, and thus innovation and growth in the US economy.

That system is now crumbling. The White House has enacted a spate of policies to restrict and repel international students en masse….

These policies, if sustained, would constrict a key supply of science and engineering talent to the US economy, reducing productivity and slowing economic growth in the long run. Using imperfect but best-available estimates from the economics research literature, we project the loss produced by a sustained decline of one third in international student inflows. Over the course of a decade, real US GDP would be smaller than it would be otherwise—an annual loss, at current GDP, of between $240 billion and $481 billion. The annual loss would be comparable to erasing the entire economy of Wisconsin or South Carolina.

The one-third reduction is extrapolated from the effects of Trump policies adopted even before this latest rule.

Clemens' article outlines the basis for these estimates in detail, and also notes additional harms that they do not consider. For example, many US universities are significantly dependent on foreign-student tuition, which is often used to subsidize financial aid for US-citizen students.

The new policies are also layered on top of Trump's unconstitutional and harmful efforts to deport foreign students who engage in political speech the administration doesn't approve of, a policy which, among other flaws, is a menace to academic freedom.

In addition, the new policies will deny valuable educational and career opportunities to many thousands of would-be students. And for no better reason than that they were born to the wrong parents in the wrong place. Conservatives rightly condemn racial and ethnic preferences in college admissions as unjust and anti-meritocratic, because they judge applicants based on morally arbitrary circumstances ancestry over which people have no control. In previous writings (see here and here), I have outlined why immigration restrictions - including discrimination against foreign students - are unjust for similar reasons, but to a much greater extent, because the effects are much larger. The difference between going to lower-tier school instead of, say, Harvard (the effect of racial affirmative action) is not as great as that between being allowed to study in the US and being categorically excluded.

The post Trump's Harmful and Unjust New Restrictions on Foreign Students appeared first on Reason.com.

articles.title: Trump's Pernicious Threat to Impose Tariffs on Canada in Response to Wildfires
articles.content:
美国东部大部分地区最近受到加拿大大规模野火烟雾的影响。这确实是一个问题。但特朗普威胁要对加拿大商品加征新关税作为回应，无助于改善局面。如果实施，将损害美国和加拿大的经济，并进一步毒化两国关系。而且这根本不可能缓解野火问题。此外，没有任何法律授权总统对这类自然灾害征收关税。

特朗普声称关税是合理的，因为“我们要让加拿大为其未能妥善维护森林及灌木丛负责，而美国正在被肮脏、污染和不健康的空气不必要地入侵。”但实际上，野火是闪电、天气模式和全球变暖共同作用的结果。无法通过“森林管理”改善来解决问题。

我认为可以争论说加拿大对火灾负有部分责任，因为它们加剧了全球变暖。但美国同样如此，而且规模要大得多（因为我们人口更多、经济体量更大）。无论如何，更高的关税不太可能导致加拿大阻止野火。

新关税真正会造成的是提高美国消费者的价格，并降低许多依赖加拿大进口作为生产投入的美国行业的生产率。例如，美国商会2025年12月的一项研究记录了特朗普此前对加拿大铝材征收关税造成的严重损害。同样，对加拿大木材征收关税会增加住房建设成本，从而进一步加剧我们的住房危机。两国的经济高度一体化，在许多领域，我们通过购买对方能够以更高质量和更低价格生产的商品而互利共赢。

新关税当然也会给加拿大带来痛苦和损失。但这本身是坏事，还可能进一步损害美国。我们有一个繁荣增长的邻国比相反的情况要好。除了其他因素之外，更富裕的加拿大可能会购买更多美国出口商品，生产更多我们可能想要购买的商品和服务，并创造更好的投资机会。

此外，新一轮关税将进一步毒化与加拿大的关系，而这一关系已经因特朗普之前的关税以及他反复威胁吞并加拿大并使其成为第51个州而受到严重损害。无端疏远我们的邻国和重要盟友不是“让美国再次伟大”。这只会让我们在需要与盟友合作对抗俄罗斯和中国等对手的时候变得更弱、更孤立。

我认识加拿大很多人，包括许多传统上对美国高度友好的保守派和自由意志主义者。由于特朗普及其政策，他们中的许多人现在与我们严重疏远，或者至少高度怀疑。而且不仅仅是那些我承认不具代表性的熟人有这种感觉。加拿大民意调查证实，对美国的愤怒和不信任大幅增长。美国人低估了破坏这一关系的危险，我们不能掉以轻心。我们不想用一个友好和配合的邻国来换取一个敌对的邻国。

最后，值得注意的是，特朗普没有法律权力因野火而征收关税。根据宪法，关税是国会的权力，而不是行政部门的权力。最高法院在废除特朗普IEEPA关税的案件中（我参与了该案诉讼）有力地重申了这一原则。而且没有任何法规授权总统对邻国的野火或其他类似自然灾害征收关税。

特朗普目前计划使用301条款关税重新实施类似IEEPA的制度。这个计划本身是非法的，彼得·哈雷尔和我在各种文章中已经概述了原因（见这里和这里）。无论如何，正如彼得详细说明的那样，301条款只能用于回应外国政府的“不公正”或“不合理或歧视性”贸易做法。野火显然不是贸易做法。

特朗普试图使用1974年贸易法第122条征收大规模新关税的努力也是如此（最近被美国国际贸易法院驳回）。第122条只能用于应对“国际收支逆差”和相关问题。野火显然不符合条件。也没有其他法规规定在这种情况下征收关税。

特朗普对IEEPA法规的解释本可以让他在这里也征收关税，因为它会赋予总统几乎任何时候想要征收关税的权力，只要他声称是回应构成“异常和非同寻常威胁”的“国家安全紧急状态”。但法院驳回了他的立场，很大程度上正是因为它给了他如此无限制的权力。

也许政府律师会开发出一些新颖的法律理论来证明在这里征收关税是正当的。但如果他们这样做了而特朗普据此行事，希望法院会予以驳回。

最终，这种情况再次提醒我们，我们不应该给一个人空白支票，让他随心所欲地征收关税。允许这样做会破坏宪法权力分立，造成巨大的经济损害，损害我们与盟友的关系，并威胁法治。

《特朗普对加拿大野火征收关税的恶毒威胁》首次发表于Reason.com。
articles.summary:
Much of the eastern United States has recently been affected by smoke originating from massive wildfires in Canada. It's a genuine problem. But Trump's threat to impose new tariffs on Canadian goods in response won't make the situation any better. If implemented, it would damage both the US and Canadian economies, and further poison relations between the two countries. And it has no chance of actually alleviating the wildfire problem. Furthermore, no law authorizes the president to impose tariffs in response to natural disasters like this one.

Trump claims the tariffs are justified because "[w]e are holding Canada responsible for the fact that they are not properly maintaining their Forests, and Brush therein, and the United States is being unnecessarily invaded by filthy, polluted, and unhealthy air." But, in reality, the fires are caused by a combination of lightning, weather patterns, and global warming. They cannot be fixed by improvement "forest management."

I suppose one can argue Canada is partly responsible for the fires because they contribute to global warming. But the same is true of the US, and on a much larger scale (because we have a much larger population and economy). In any event, higher tariffs aren't likely to somehow lead Canada to prevent the wildfires.

What new tariffs would do is increase prices for American consumers, and reduce the productivity of the many US industries that rely on Canadian imports as inputs in their production process. For example, a December 2025 study by the US Chamber of Commerce documents the great harm caused by Trump's earlier tariffs on Canadian aluminum.  Similarly, tariffs on Canadian lumber increase the cost of housing construction, thereby further exacerbating our housing crisis. The two countries' economies are highly integrated, and there are many sectors where we mutually benefit by purchasing goods that the other country can produce at higher quality and lower prices.

New tariffs will also, of course, cause pain and suffering in Canada. But that is both bad in itself, and likely to further damage  the US. It's better for us to have a prosperous and growing neighbor than the opposite. Among other things, a wealthier Canada is likely to purchase more US exports, produce more goods and services we might want to buy, and generate better investment opportunities.

In addition, a new wave of tariffs would further poison relations with Canada, which have already been seriously damaged by Trump's previous tariffs and his repeated threats to annex Canada and make it the 51st state. Gratuitously alienating our neighbor and vital ally isn't "Making American Great Again." It's making us weaker and more isolated at a time when we need to work with allies to counter adversaries like Russia and China.

I know many people in Canada, including a good many conservatives and libertarians traditionally highly sympathetic to the US. Thanks to Trump and his policies, many of them are now deeply alienated from us, or at least highly suspicious. And it's not just a my admittedly unrepresentative acquaintances who feel that way.  Polls of Canadian opinion confirm a massive growth in anger and distrust of the United States. Americans underestimate the dangers of ruining this relationship at our peril. We don't want to replace a friendly and accommodating neighbor with a hostile one.

Finally, it's worth noting that Trump does not have the legal authority to impose tariffs in response to wildfires. Under the Constitution, tariffs are a congressional power, not an executive one. The Supreme Court forcefully reaffirmed that principle when it invalidated Trump's IEEPA tariffs (in a case I helped litigate). And there is no statute authorizing the president to impose tariffs in response to wildfires or other similar natural disasters in a neighboring country.

Trump is currently planning to reimpose something like the IEEPA regime using Section 301 tariffs. That plan is itself illegal for reasons Peter Harrell and I have outlined in various writings (see here and here). In any event, as Peter details, Section 301 can only be used in response to "unjustifiable" or "unreasonable or discriminatory" trade practices by foreign governments. Wildfires are pretty obviously not a trade practice.

The same goes for Trump's efforts to impose massive new tariffs using Section 122 of the Trade Act of 1974 (recently struck down by the US Court of International Trade). Section 122 can only be used to counter "balance of payment deficits" and related problems. Wildfires pretty obviously don't qualify. Nor is there any other statute that provides for tariffs in such situations.

Trump's interpretation of the IEEPA statute would have allowed him to impose tariffs here, as well, since it would give the president the power to impose tariffs virtually anytime he wants, so long has he claims it is in response to a "national emergency" that amounts to an "unusual and extraordinary threat." But the courts rejected his position in large part precisely because it gave him such unlimited power.

Perhaps Administration lawyers will yet develop some novel legal theory to try to justify the imposition of tariffs here. But, if they do and Trump acts on it, hopefully courts will reject it.

Ultimately, this situation is yet another reminder that we should not give one man a blank check to impose tariffs whenever he feels like it. Allowing that undermines the constitutional separation of powers, causes great economic harm, damages our relations with allies, and menaces the rule of law.

 

 

 

The post Trump's Pernicious Threat to Impose Tariffs on Canada in Response to Wildfires appeared first on Reason.com.
